body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes (permission_hooks exist — in the TUI)
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: gap:permission-approval

## Scenario

tool-gate-permission-prompt: the agent invokes a tool that triggers a permission prompt; the session enters `waiting` and stays there until the user resolves the prompt (approve/deny). The user-observable signal (matching the claudecode row) is the arc `ready → working → waiting`, with final state `waiting` while the prompt is open — and, with the approve/deny half exercised, the waiting → working/ready resolution.

## Agent supports it (agent_supports: yes)

opencode declares `permission_hooks: true` (replaydata/agents/opencode/capabilities.json). opencode's interactive TUI does gate tool execution behind a permission prompt (a per-tool ask overlay the user approves or denies). So the agent the scenario targets CAN, in its TUI, surface the permission gate this cell asserts.

## Why this cell is NOT recordable today (driver gap: permission-approval)

The permission gate lives ENTIRELY in the interactive TUI. The recording rig drives opencode through the headless `opencode run` path, which AUTO-EXECUTES tools inside opencode's own sandbox with NO interactive permission prompt — there is no `waiting` episode to capture. PROVEN this sweep: the opencode/auto-executed-tool-call recording captured the local gemma model's `write` tool running automatically INSIDE the working span with zero waiting/approval state (ready → working → ready, no waiting). So a tool-gate permission prompt simply does not arise on the headless driver.

Even if it did, the driver could not exercise the cell's second half: `drive-opencode-interactive.sh` drives opencode through BLOCKING headless `opencode run` subprocesses and implements ONLY `send` / `wait_turn` (a no-op — `opencode run` already blocks until the turn ends) / `sleep` in its `case "$TYPE"` dispatch. There is NO `approve`/`deny` step (and no `keys` step) to RESOLVE a permission prompt, so even with a prompt held open the waiting → approve/deny transition the cell asserts is unreachable. The recipe is left `applicable:false` with no `script`, so recipe-lint.sh cannot flag this (no step-TYPE to reject); the missing capability is a live-REPL permission-approval primitive, the same shape as the opencode user-esc-interrupt (gap:interrupt) and model-switch-midsession (gap:model-select) driver-gap cells that recipe-lint likewise could not catch.

## daemon side

opencode is the lone `ProcessOwnedStore` adapter (core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/agent.go) — the daemon reads session/turn state from the SQLite store, not from hooks. The store-reading parser/watcher have no permission-gate handling and no `waiting` emission path (grep of parser.go / watcher.go finds no permission/waiting branch); the canonical `waiting` gate (rule 0 PermissionPending) is a claudecode-hook path that opencode's store transport does not feed. The verdict is recorded as daemon_capability: full per the routing decision (the blocker is the driver, not the daemon), but the daemon's observability of an opencode permission `waiting` episode is UNPROVEN — it cannot be tested until a live-REPL driver can elicit and resolve the TUI prompt. If/when unblocked, the live recording may correct daemon_capability downward (a likely observability_correction on this ProcessOwnedStore transport).

## What would unblock it

Replace or augment `drive-opencode-interactive.sh` with a LIVE-REPL driver (analogous to the tmux-based claudecode/pi/aider drivers): launch the opencode TUI in a tmux pane, `send` a tool-inducing prompt so the permission overlay opens (capturing the `waiting` episode), then fire an `approve`/`deny` step that resolves the prompt and lets the turn settle. Only then can the cell be recorded; and only then can the daemon's observability of the opencode permission gate be confirmed.

## Confidence

0.85: the driver grammar (`send|wait_turn|sleep`, blocking `opencode run` per send, no approve/deny step) is read directly from the live driver source, the headless auto-execute-no-prompt behavior is proven by the sibling auto-executed-tool-call recording, and the absence of any permission/waiting branch in the store-reading parser/watcher is read directly from source. Not higher only because the unblocked recording (a live TUI permission prompt resolved through to a state settle) has not been captured — that is the developer task this gap defers.
caveats: Driver-gap, not an agent limitation: opencode declares permission_hooks:true and its TUI gates tools behind an approve/deny prompt. The blocker is the headless driver — `opencode run` auto-executes tools with no prompt (proven by auto-executed-tool-call), and the driver has no approve/deny step to resolve one even if it appeared.

recipe-lint.sh does NOT catch this gap: the recipe is left applicable:false with no script, so there is no step-TYPE to reject; the missing capability is a live-REPL permission-approval primitive (same shape as the opencode user-esc-interrupt gap:interrupt / model-switch-midsession gap:model-select cells).

daemon_capability:full is the routing label (the driver is the blocker), but UNPROVEN: opencode's store-reading parser/watcher have no permission-gate or waiting branch, so whether a permission waiting episode is observable from the SQLite store cannot be tested until a live-REPL driver elicits one. A likely observability_correction if/when recorded.

sources.note: case "$TYPE" handles only send|wait_turn|sleep; each `send` launches a BLOCKING `opencode run` subprocess (headless) that auto-executes tools with no permission prompt — no approve/deny/keys step to elicit or resolve a permission gate
permission_hooks:true and tool_calls:true — opencode supports a tool permission gate, but it lives in the interactive TUI, not the headless `opencode run` path the rig drives

PROOF the headless driver auto-executes tools with no prompt: the auto-executed-tool-call cell captured gemma's `write` tool running inside the working span with zero waiting/approval state (ready→working→ready, no waiting)

store-reading parser has NO permission/ask/approve/deny/waiting branch — the canonical rule-0 PermissionPending→waiting gate is a claudecode-hook path opencode's ProcessOwnedStore transport does not feed
opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter; daemon reads session/turn state from SQLite, so observability of a permission waiting episode is unproven until a live-REPL driver can elicit one
the recordable claudecode sibling: ready→working→waiting via a PreToolUse permissionDecision=ask hook (rule 0 PermissionPending→waiting) — the target arc opencode cannot reach headlessly
